turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Thomas has the opinion of the Court in Case 24-320, Soto v. United States.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: This case comes to us on a writ of certiorari to the United States Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit. The Barring Act establishes default settlement procedures for claims against the government and subjects most claims to a six-year limitations period. But the Act includes an exception. If another law confers authority to settle a claim against the government, that law displaces the Barring Act settlement mechanism, including its limitations period. This case is about whether a statute providing Combat-Related Special Compensation, known as CRSC, to qualifying veterans is another law that displaces the Barring Act's default settlement mechanism. Petitioner Simon Soto is a military veteran who applied for CRSC payments in 2016. He was granted retroactive compensation under the statute, but was awarded only six years, worth of benefits because of the Barring Act's limitations period. Soto sued, arguing that this limitations period did not apply to CRSC claims because the CRSC statute provides its own settlement mechanism and, therefore, displaces the Barring Act. The District Court granted summary judgment to Soto, but the Federal Circuit reversed. In the Federal Circuit's view, the CRSC statute does not explicitly grant settlement authority and, therefore, cannot displace the Barring Act settlement procedures. In an opinion filed with the Clerk today, we reverse.In the context of claims against the government, the authorization to settle a claim requires authority to determine the validity of a claim and the amount of money that the claimant is due. The CRSC statute is a unique, comprehensive benefits regime that confers authority to conduct these core settlement functions. For these reasons, and others set forth in our opinion, we reverse the judgment of the Federal Circuit. The opinion of the Court is unanimous.
